#REDIRECT[[群集墳]]

'''古墳群'''（こふんぐん）とは、複数の[[古墳]]によって構成される群のことである。
== 概要 == [[Image:Makimuku kohungun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|[[纒向古墳群]]のなかの勝山古墳（上）・矢塚古墳（下左）・石塚古墳（下右）（{{国土航空写真}}）]] 古墳は、1基で単独に存在する例はそれほど多くなく、通常、数基または数十基で群を構成することがむしろ一般的であり、これを「古墳群」と呼称している。なお、古墳群を構成する古墳が数百基におよぶ例もある。著名な古墳群に、[[大阪府]]の[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]、奈良県の[[大和古墳群]]・[[纒向古墳群]]・[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]、[[宮崎県]]の[[西都原古墳群]]などがある。
古墳群を構成せず、他の古墳から離れて1基単独で存在する場合、その古墳を「単独墳」と呼ぶ場合がある。また、1つの古墳群のなかで、他と隔絶した規模・施設をもつ古墳を「盟主墳」と呼称する場合もあるが、いずれも慣用的な用法である。
古墳群のあり方は、当該古墳群の所在する地域における古墳時代の歴史的経緯や諸相をよくあらわしている。[[円墳]]のみで構成される古墳群、何基かの[[前方後円墳]]を含む古墳群、中期古墳のみの古墳群、前期から後期まで連続して営まれた古墳群など、そのあり方は古墳群の営まれた地域の様相や系譜をともにする被葬者たる[[氏族]]の消長、その権力の性格などを端的に示している。なお、とくに古墳時代後期において、小規模な円墳がごく狭小範囲に群集して分布する場合には、古墳群ではなく「[[群集墳]]」と呼び、たがいに区別している。
== 関連項目 == *[[群集墳]]
== 参考文献 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1
{{DEFAULTSORT:こふんくん}} [[Category:古墳|*]] [[Category:考古学]]

'''古墳群'''（こふんぐん）とは、複数の[[古墳]]によって構成される群のことである。

古墳群を構成せず、他の古墳から離れて1基単独で存在する場合、その古墳を「単独墳」と呼ぶ場合がある。また、1つの古墳群のなかで、他と隔絶した規模・施設をもつ古墳を「盟主墳」と呼称する場合もあるが、いずれも慣用的な用法である。
古墳群のあり方は、当該古墳群の所在する地域における古墳時代の歴史的経緯や諸相をよくあらわしている。[[円墳]]のみで構成される古墳群、何基かの[[前方後円墳]]を含む古墳群、中期古墳のみの古墳群、前期から後期まで連続して営まれた古墳群など、そのあり方は古墳群の営まれた地域の様相や系譜をともにする被葬者たる[[氏族]]の消長、その権力の性格などを端的に示している。なお、とくに古墳時代後期において、小規模な円墳がごく狭小範囲に群集して分布する場合には、古墳群ではなく「[[群集墳]]」と呼び、たがいに区別している。
== 関連項目 == *[[群集墳]]
== 参考文献 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1
{{DEFAULTSORT:こふんくん}} [[Category:古墳|*]] [[Category:考古学]]

== 参考文献 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1

'''古墳群'''（こふんぐん）とは、複数の[[古墳]]によって構成される群のことである。
== 概要 == [[Image:Makimuku kohungun aerial.jpg|thumb|240px|[[纒向古墳群]]のなかの勝山古墳（上）・矢塚古墳（下左）・石塚古墳（下右）（{{国土航空写真}}）]] 古墳は、1基で単独に存在する例はそれほど多くなく、通常、数基または数十基で群を構成することがむしろ一般的であり、これを「古墳群」と呼称している。なお、古墳群を構成する古墳が数百基におよぶ例もある。著名な古墳群に、[[大阪府]]の[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]、奈良県の[[大和古墳群]]・[[纒向古墳群]]・[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]・[[馬見古墳群]]、和歌山県の[[岩橋千塚古墳群]]、[[宮崎県]]の[[西都原古墳群]]などがある。

[[Image:Makimuku kohungun aerial.jpg|thumb|200px|[[纒向古墳群]]のなかの勝山古墳（上）・矢塚古墳（下左）・石塚古墳（下右）（{{国土航空写真}}）]]
'''古墳群'''（こふんぐん）とは、[[考古学]]における[[遺跡]]呼称の1つで、複数の[[古墳]]によって構成される[[遺跡群]]である{{Sfn|奈良文化財研究所|2020|p=8}}。
== 概要 == 古墳は、1基で単独に存在する例はそれほど多くなく、通常、数基または数十基で群を構成することがむしろ一般的であり、これを「古墳群」という。また、古墳群を構成する古墳が数百基におよぶ例もある。著名な古墳群に、[[大阪府]]の[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]、奈良県の[[大和古墳群]]・[[纒向古墳群]]・[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]・[[馬見古墳群]]、[[和歌山県]]の[[岩橋千塚古墳群]]、[[宮崎県]]の[[西都原古墳群]]などがある。

古墳群のあり方は、当該古墳群の所在する地域における古墳時代の歴史的経緯や諸相をよくあらわしている。[[円墳]]のみで構成される古墳群、何基かの[[前方後円墳]]を含む古墳群、中期古墳のみの古墳群、前期から後期まで連続して営まれた古墳群など、そのあり方は古墳群の営まれた地域の様相や系譜をともにする被葬者たる[[氏族]]の消長、その権力の性格などを端的に示している。なお、特に[[古墳時代]]後期において見られる、小規模な円墳がごく狭小範囲に群集して分布する場合は、古墳群の中でも「[[群集墳]]」と呼ぶ。
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1 *{{Cite book|和書|author=奈良文化財研究所|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=遺跡情報の構造|title=遺跡データベース簡易版|publisher=奈良文化財研究所|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== 関連項目 == *[[群集墳]]
{{考古学}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こふんくん}} [[Category:古墳|*]] [[Category:古墳群|*]] [[Category:考古学]]

[[Image:Makimuku kohungun aerial.jpg|thumb|200px|[[纒向古墳群]]のなかの勝山古墳（上）・矢塚古墳（下左）・石塚古墳（下右）（{{国土航空写真}}）]]
'''古墳群'''（こふんぐん）とは、[[考古学]]における[[遺跡]]呼称の1つで、複数の[[古墳]]によって構成される[[遺跡群]]である{{Sfn|奈良文化財研究所|2020|p=8}}。
== 概要 == 古墳は、1基で単独に存在する例はそれほど多くなく、通常、数基または数十基で群を構成することがむしろ一般的であり、これを「古墳群」という。また、古墳群を構成する古墳が数百基におよぶ例もある。著名な古墳群に、[[大阪府]]の[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]、奈良県の[[大和古墳群]]・[[纒向古墳群]]・[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]・[[馬見古墳群]]、[[和歌山県]]の[[岩橋千塚古墳群]]、[[宮崎県]]の[[西都原古墳群]]などがある。
古墳群を構成せず、他の古墳から離れて1基単独で存在する場合、その古墳を「単独墳」と呼ぶ場合がある。また、1つの古墳群のなかで、他と隔絶した規模・施設をもつ古墳を「盟主墳」と呼称する場合もあるが、いずれも慣用的な用法である。
古墳群のあり方は、当該古墳群の所在する地域における古墳時代の歴史的経緯や諸相をよくあらわしている。[[円墳]]のみで構成される古墳群、何基かの[[前方後円墳]]を含む古墳群、中期古墳のみの古墳群、前期から後期まで連続して営まれた古墳群など、そのあり方は古墳群の営まれた地域の様相や系譜をともにする被葬者たる[[氏族]]の消長、その権力の性格などを端的に示している。なお、特に[[古墳時代]]後期において見られる、小規模な円墳がごく狭小範囲に群集して分布する場合は、古墳群の中でも「[[群集墳]]」と呼ぶ。
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1 *{{Cite book|和書|author=奈良文化財研究所|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=遺跡情報の構造|title=遺跡データベース簡易版|publisher=奈良文化財研究所|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== 関連項目 == *[[群集墳]] *[[伽倻古墳群]] - 韓国の古墳群 {{考古学}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こふんくん}} [[Category:古墳|*]] [[Category:古墳群|*]] [[Category:考古学]]

[[Image:Makimuku kohungun aerial.jpg|thumb|200px|[[纒向古墳群]]のなかの勝山古墳（上）・矢塚古墳（下左）・石塚古墳（下右）（{{国土航空写真}}）]]

revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kofun group]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwasaki Chidzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli Cluster|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.
{{Archaeology}} [[Category:Kofun|*]] [[Category:Kofun Clusters|*]] [[Category:Archaeology]] [[ja:古墳群]]

The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Kofun groups]] to [[Kofun cluster]]
revision.text:
[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwasaki Chidzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli Cluster|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.
{{Archaeology}} [[Category:Kofun|*]] [[Category:Kofun Clusters|*]] [[Category:Archaeology]] [[Category:Original page]] [[Category:Pages to try to fix ills]] [[ja:古墳群]]

When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: attributing pages to myself
revision.text:
[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun clusters]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:古墳群]]

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "佐紀盾列古墳群}}" to "佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}}"
revision.text:
[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient burial mounds—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single burial mound" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳|1=Gunshufun}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳|1=Gunshufun}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}} == See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳|1=Gunshufun}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Kofun Cluster|ja|百舌鳥古墳群|1=Mozu Tombs|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|ja|古市古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Group|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|ja|纒向古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=draft|12=draft}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|ja|岩橋千塚古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|ja|西都原古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳|1=Gunshufun|12=draft|12=draft}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|ja|伽倻古墳群|12=draft|12=draft}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

revision.text:
[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|[[纒向古墳群]]のなかの勝山古墳（上）・矢塚古墳（下左）・石塚古墳（下右）（{{国土航空写真}}）]]
'''古墳群'''（こふんぐん）とは、[[考古学]]における[[遺跡]]呼称の1つで、複数の[[古墳]]によって構成される[[遺跡群]]である{{Sfn|奈良文化財研究所|2020|p=8}}。
== 概要 == 古墳は、1基で単独に存在する例はそれほど多くなく、通常、数基または数十基で群を構成することがむしろ一般的であり、これを「古墳群」という。また、古墳群を構成する古墳が数百基におよぶ例もある。著名な古墳群に、[[大阪府]]の[[百舌鳥古墳群]]・[[古市古墳群]]、奈良県の[[大和古墳群]]・[[纒向古墳群]]・[[佐紀盾列古墳群]]・[[馬見古墳群]]、[[和歌山県]]の[[岩橋千塚古墳群]]、[[宮崎県]]の[[西都原古墳群]]などがある。
古墳群を構成せず、他の古墳から離れて1基単独で存在する場合、その古墳を「単独墳」と呼ぶ場合がある。また、1つの古墳群のなかで、他と隔絶した規模・施設をもつ古墳を「盟主墳」と呼称する場合もあるが、いずれも慣用的な用法である。
古墳群のあり方は、当該古墳群の所在する地域における古墳時代の歴史的経緯や諸相をよくあらわしている。[[円墳]]のみで構成される古墳群、何基かの[[前方後円墳]]を含む古墳群、中期古墳のみの古墳群、前期から後期まで連続して営まれた古墳群など、そのあり方は古墳群の営まれた地域の様相や系譜をともにする被葬者たる[[氏族]]の消長、その権力の性格などを端的に示している。なお、特に[[古墳時代]]後期において見られる、小規模な円墳がごく狭小範囲に群集して分布する場合は、古墳群の中でも「[[群集墳]]」と呼ぶ。
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1 *{{Cite book|和書|author=奈良文化財研究所|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=遺跡情報の構造|title=遺跡データベース簡易版|publisher=奈良文化財研究所|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== 関連項目 == *[[群集墳]] *[[伽倻古墳群]] - 韓国の古墳群。2023年に世界文化遺産に登録。 {{考古学}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こふんくん}} [[Category:古墳|*]] [[Category:古墳群|*]] [[Category:考古学]]

'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]]—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}

== Overview == 
Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]] and [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]], {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture, the [[:en:Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Wakayama Prefecture, and the [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in Miyazaki Prefecture.

When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.

The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.

== References ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}

'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]]—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}

== Overview == 
Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]] and [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]], {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture, the [[:en:Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Wakayama Prefecture, and the [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in Miyazaki Prefecture.

When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.

The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.

== References ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}

[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]] ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Aerial Photography|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397}})]]

'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]]—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}

== Overview == 
Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the [[:en:Mozu Tombs|Mozu Tombs]] and [[:en:Furuichi Kofun Cluster|Furuichi Kofun Cluster]] in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, [[:en:Makimuku Kofun Cluster|Makimuku Kofun Cluster]], {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture, the [[:en:Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster]] in Wakayama Prefecture, and the [[:en:Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster]] in Miyazaki Prefecture.

When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.

The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.

== References ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳|1=gunshūfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunshufun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunshufun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609514}} (concentrated kofun clusters)
* [[:en:Gaya Tumuli|Gaya Tumuli]] - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:古墳群]]
[[Image:Three Makimuku kofun aerial view.jpg|thumb|200px|Katsuyama Kofun (top), Yazuka Kofun (bottom left), and Ishizuka Kofun (bottom right) within the {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|en|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11608501|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|National Aerial Photography|ja|国土航空写真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Aerial Photography|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491397}})]]
'''Kofun Clusters''' (古墳群, ''Kofungun'') are groups of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}—ancient Kofuns—constituting a type of archaeological site.{{Sfn|Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|2020|p=8}}
== Overview == Kofun rarely exist in isolation; it is more common for them to appear in groups of several to dozens of mounds, which are collectively referred to as "kofun clusters." Some clusters comprise hundreds of kofun. Notable examples include the {{ill|Mozu Tombs|en|Mozu Tombs|WD=Q4250584|lt=Mozu Tombs}} and {{ill|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|en|Furuichi Kofun Cluster|WD=Q4250076|lt=Furuichi Kofun Cluster}} in Osaka Prefecture, the {{ill|Yamato Kofun Cluster|ja|大和古墳群|1=Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamato Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, {{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|en|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11608501|lt=Makimuku Kofun Cluster}}, {{ill|Saki Kofun Cluster|ja|佐紀盾列古墳群|1=Saki Tatenami Kofun group|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Saki Tatenami Kofun group|qq=}}, and {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}} in Nara Prefecture, the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11475228|lt=Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster}} in Wakayama Prefecture, and the {{ill|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|en|Saitobaru Kofun Cluster|WD=Q3469314|lt=Saitobaru Kofun Cluster}} in Miyazaki Prefecture.
When a kofun exists in isolation, unassociated with any cluster, it may be referred to as a "single Kofun" or ''tandokufun'' (単独墳). Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
The arrangement and composition of kofun clusters often reflect the cultural, political, or geographical characteristics of the regions in which they are located.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=Ōtsuka Hatsushige, Kobayashi Saburō|title=Kofun Jiten|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1982|date=December 1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|url=https://sitereports.nabunken.go.jp/files/statics/10IsekiJohoKozo.pdf|chapter=The Structure of Archaeological Site Information|title=Simplified Archaeological Site Database|publisher=Nara National Research Institute for Cultural Properties|year=2020|date=2020-06-12|ref=harv}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Gunshufun|ja|群集墳|1=gunshūfun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunshufun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunshufun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11609514}} (concentrated kofun clusters) * {{ill|Gaya Tumuli|en|Gaya Tumuli|WD=Q66201394|lt=Gaya Tumuli}} - A cluster of ancient tombs in Korea, registered as a World Cultural Heritage site in 2023.
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|古墳群|version=99047723|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Within a kofun cluster, a particularly large or elaborate mound may be designated as a "principal mound" or ''meishufun'' (盟主墳), though these terms are used informally.
